prompt_md: Why must function template definitions be placed in header files (or otherwise included at every call site)?

options.a: Headers are compiled before `.cpp` files, so templates must be there first.
options.b: The compiler needs the full template definition to instantiate it at each call site.
options.c: The linker cannot merge template instantiations from different object files.
options.d: Templates are interpreted at runtime and headers are loaded at startup.

explanation_md: A function template is not code — it is a recipe. The compiler must see the complete recipe at the point where it processes a call like `add(3, 4)` in order to stamp out `add<int>`. If the definition is in a separate `.cpp` file the compiler has already finished, the recipe is unavailable and instantiation fails. The linker *does* merge duplicate instantiations (option c is wrong); the problem is upstream at compile time, not link time.

`doubled(7)` instantiates `doubled<int>`, returning `7 * 2 = 14`. `doubled(2.5)` instantiates `doubled<double>`, returning `2.5 * 2 = 5.0`, which `cout` prints as `5` without a decimal point under default formatting.

prompt_md: A project has `template <typename T> T square(T x);` in `utils.h` and `template <typename T> T square(T x) { return x * x; }` in `utils.cpp`. `main.cpp` includes `utils.h` and calls `square(4)`. What is the most likely outcome?

options.a: Compiles and links fine — the linker finds the definition in `utils.cpp`.
options.b: Compile error in `utils.cpp` because template definitions are not allowed in `.cpp` files.
options.d: Runtime error because the template was not instantiated with `int`.

explanation_md: The definition lives in `utils.cpp` and is never instantiated there because nothing in `utils.cpp` calls `square`. When `main.cpp` calls `square(4)`, the compiler sees only the declaration and cannot instantiate it. The resulting object file has an unresolved reference to `square<int>`, which the linker then reports as an error.

options.a: `helper.h` should not contain a declaration — only the definition.
options.b: The definition of `halve` is in `helper.cpp` so `main.cpp` never sees it when compiling, and `halve<int>` is never instantiated.
options.c: `halve` must be marked `inline` to be used across translation units.

explanation_md: The root cause is that the template definition is hidden in `helper.cpp`. When the compiler processes `main.cpp`, it only sees the declaration and cannot instantiate `halve<int>`. The object file for `helper.cpp` also never instantiates `halve<int>` because nothing calls it there. The linker is then left with an unresolved symbol. The fix is to move the definition into `helper.h`.
